authors: Narayan, R. and Narayan, D. and Mistry, J.
keywords: capital mobility;Feldstein-Horioka puzzle;Pacific Island countries;time-varying coefficients
notes: RAYYAN-INCLUSION: {"Roxane"=>"Included"} | RAYYAN-LABELS: Fiji,Nauru,Samoa,Tonga,Tuvalu,Vanuatu,Kiribati,Micronesia,Cook Islands,Macro-economics,Solomon Islands,capital mobility,Papua New Guinea (PNG),Pacific Island Countries,Feldstein–Horioka puzzle,current account sustainability,time-varying cointegration model | USER-NOTES: {"Roxane"=>["The current account is a country's balance with the rest of the world: exports minus imports, plus income flows, plus transfers like remittances and aid. A deficit means the country spends more abroad than it earns, and the gap has to be financed somehow, by borrowing, foreign investment, aid, or remittances coming in. Sustainability asks whether that gap can keep being financed indefinitely without ending in a debt crisis, a currency crash, or forced austerity. A deficit financed by stable inflows can run for decades; one financed by short-term borrowing cannot. This is very SIDS-relevant: most Pacific islands import far more than they export and run permanent deficits, sustained precisely by aid and remittances. That's why the paper links the two, it's asking whether those external flows make the position durable or mask a vulnerability."]}

notes: RAYYAN-INCLUSION: {"Roxane"=>"Included"} | RAYYAN-LABELS: Aid,Fiji,Japan,EU aid,Australia,aid policy,france aid,Foreign aid,germany aid,income growth,aid allocation,Household income,aid effectiveness,japan foreign aid,foreign aid delivery,financial development,Pacific Island Countries,Autoregressive distributed lag (ARDL),Korea International Cooperation Agency | USER-NOTES: {"Roxane"=>["procurement policy: the legal framework governing how a government buys goods, services, and works, things like competitive tendering, transparency, and anti-corruption safeguards. Fiji introduced a formal one in 2010. The paper's logic is that donors care how well a recipient country manages public money, so stronger procurement rules lower the fiduciary risk of their aid being misused, which makes them more willing to give. They test that with an ARDL time-series model and find that procurement legislation and financial development have a long-run positive association with aid inflows (positive for Australia, the EU, Germany, and total aid; Korea only in the short run), while crisis and political uncertainty depress them."]}

notes: RAYYAN-INCLUSION: {"Roxane"=>"Included"} | RAYYAN-LABELS: methods,Remittance,econometrics,measuring remittances using different methodologies | USER-NOTES: {"Roxane"=>["EA uses a model to estimate remittances from the United States and, although the methodology has some strengths, the accuracy of BEA’s estimate is uncertain for several reasons. BEA estimated remittances for 2003 at $28.2 billion; its model used data on the number of foreign-born residents, their income, the proportion of income that is remitted, and other demographic data. The strengths of BEA’s methodology are that, in theory, it estimates remittances sent through formal and informal channels. It also is low-cost because it uses existing data on the foreign born. However, BEA’s methodology was limited by the quality and timeliness of the data, particularly on the portion of income likely to be remitted. BEA revised its model in 2005 to use new data sources, but the accuracy of its estimates depends on the accuracy of its assumptions regarding the remitting behavior of the foreign born and other factors."]}

Natural disasters occur in a political space.
